risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Treatment assignments for randomization were generated in SAS by the study statistician using permuted blocks with randomly-varying block sizes. Randomization was stratified by surgery type." Comment: This describes a clearly random allocation sequence generated by a statistician using SAS, with permuted blocks and varying block sizes, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Treatment assignments for randomization were generated in SAS by the study statistician using permuted blocks with randomly-varying block sizes. Treatment assignments were sealed in sequentially-numbered, opaque envelopes. Randomization was stratified by surgery type. Study staff opened sealed envelopes immediately after baseline testing to determine the final treatment assignment." Comment: The study explicitly reports sequentially-numbered, opaque envelopes used to hold treatment assignments until after baseline testing, which is a concealment mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment by the enrolling staff.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The trial compared a 10-week mindfulness-based group intervention with a 1-hour individualized nutritional counseling session, and the methods do not describe any blinding of participants or intervention personnel. Because participants and the instructor/dietician necessarily knew which intervention they were receiving/delivering, and the outcomes included self-reported eating behavior, depression, stress, coping, and quality of life that could plausibly be influenced by this knowledge, the study is at high risk of performance bias overall. Comment: Objective outcomes like weight and biomarkers were also measured, but the dominant outcome set was subjective and likely susceptible to lack of blinding.

Summary: The study does not explicitly report blinding of outcome assessors. Outcomes were assessed by mixed methods: patient self-report questionnaires (TFEQ-R18, BES, WEL, SF-36, IWQOL-Lite, CES-D, PSS, Brief COPE), direct anthropometric measurement (weight, waist circumference, BMI), biochemical assays (HbA1C, adiponectin, hs-CRP, IL-6, TNF-a), and qualitative interviews/coding by study staff. The methods state only that "Study outcomes were assessed at baseline, 12 weeks, and 6 months" and describe that "the primary intervention developer and instructor (SC) administered the interview" and that "Three study coders ... read the interviews," but there is no statement that outcome assessors were blinded. Because the outcome set includes subjective self-reported outcomes and judgement-based qualitative outcomes, lack of information on assessor blinding leaves detection bias unclear.

Quote: "Adherence and retention were excellent. Six of nine patients attended ≥ 7 of 10 mindfulness classes; two patients attended < 5 classes due to unexpected life circumstances. 100% completed all study follow-up visits and were included in the intention-to-treat analysis." Comment: Follow-up was complete for all randomized participants, with no missing outcome data at the study follow-up visits. Although there was some intervention non-adherence in the mindfulness arm, this does not indicate attrition bias because all participants completed follow-up and were included in the ITT analysis.
